intro: Authority is not a flat pile. It is layered, and the layers decide which source wins when two of them point in different directions. This page lays out three things you have to hold in your head before you can rank anything: the vertical hierarchy of authority, the line between primary and secondary sources, and the rule that the same case can be binding in one courtroom and merely persuasive in another.

pyramid.lede: Within a single system, higher law controls lower law. Federal law, where it applies, sits above state law (the Supremacy Clause). The pyramid is the textbook’s Figure 1.2 — read it top to bottom.

levels.label: U.S. Constitution
levels.note: The supreme law of the land. Any law that conflicts with it is invalid.

levels.label: Federal statutes & treaties
levels.note: Enacted by Congress (or made under its treaty power). Bind nationwide within federal authority.

levels.label: Federal regulations
levels.note: Issued by agencies under a statute. Valid only within the authority the statute grants.

levels.label: Federal case law
levels.note: Federal courts interpreting federal law; binding effect runs down each circuit’s own ladder.

levels.label: State constitution
levels.note: Supreme within the state — so long as it does not conflict with federal law.

levels.label: State statutes
levels.note: Enacted by the state legislature; outrank state regulations and state common law on the same subject.

levels.label: State regulations
levels.note: Issued by state agencies under a state statute.

levels.label: State case law
levels.note: State courts interpreting state law; binding effect runs down that state’s own ladder.

levels.label: Local ordinances
levels.note: City and county rules, valid within the powers the state grants them.

pyramid.note: Two cautions the chapter stresses: where federal law sets only a floor (much environmental and labor law), a state may go stricter — so check preemption before you assume federal law wins. And being struck down is not the same as being erased: an unconstitutional statute usually still appears in the code, findable but unenforceable.

primarySecondary.body: Primary authority is the law itself — constitutions, statutes, regulations, cases, treaties. Secondary sources explain, summarize, or analyze the law without being the law: treatises, legal encyclopedias, law review articles, and Restatements. Think of secondary sources as the road map and primary authority as the road. You read the map to find the road, then you travel the road. In practice you usually start in secondary sources and end in primary authority — and you cite primary authority, not the map, when it counts.
primarySecondary.callout: A Restatement reads like a code and is enormously influential, but it is still secondary — a court is never required to follow it. Influence is not the same as binding force.

forumExplorers.eyebrow: Binding vs. persuasive — it depends on the court
forumExplorers.heading: The same source can bind — or not
forumExplorers.lede: Whether a primary authority binds depends on which court is deciding and what kind of question is before it. Switch between the two Arizona courts below and watch how the same decisions change weight.

forums.label: Arizona Supreme Court (a federal constitutional question)

authorities.label: Arizona Supreme Court decision (on Arizona law)

why.az_sup: The Arizona Supreme Court is the highest court in the state system. Its decisions bind every Arizona court below it.
why.az_supreme_con: A court is not bound by its own prior decisions — it retains the power to overrule itself. Prior Arizona Supreme Court decisions are powerfully persuasive but the court may depart from them.

why.az_sup: The Arizona Court of Appeals sits directly above the Arizona Superior Court in the same system. Its published decisions bind the trial court below.
why.az_supreme_con: Binding force runs from higher courts to lower courts, never upward. The Arizona Supreme Court is above the Court of Appeals; a lower court cannot bind the court above it.

authorities.label: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit (a federal question)

why.az_sup: Federal circuit courts do not bind state courts. On an Arizona-law question, the Ninth Circuit’s views are informative but not mandatory.
why.az_supreme_con: State courts follow the U.S. Supreme Court on the meaning of the federal Constitution — not the circuit courts. The Ninth Circuit’s constitutional reasoning may inform the Arizona Supreme Court but does not control it.

authorities.label: U.S. District Court for a District in California (a federal question)

why.az_sup: A federal trial court in another state has no authority in Arizona’s system. Its decisions are persuasive at most and rarely carried this far.
why.az_supreme_con: Federal district court decisions are not binding precedent even within the federal system. For the Arizona Supreme Court, a California district court decision is at best a persuasive data point.

forumExplorers.heading: Now switch the question — and the court
forumExplorers.lede: Here the same authorities appear before two very different courts: one applying state law, one applying a federal regulation. Some sources bind both courts; most bind only one. Watch which flip and which hold steady.

forums.label: Texas state trial court (a Texas-law question)
Georgia Court of Appeals (applying a federal regulation)

authorities.label: Texas Supreme Court decision (on Texas law)

why.tx_state: The Texas Supreme Court is the highest civil court in Texas. Its decisions bind all Texas courts below it on Texas-law questions.
why.ga_app: Texas precedent carries no authority in Georgia. A Georgia court applying federal regulation looks to federal courts and Georgia’s own courts — not another state’s supreme court.

authorities.label: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit (interpreting the federal regulation)

why.tx_state: The Fifth Circuit governs federal courts in Texas — not state courts deciding state-law questions. Federal circuit precedent does not bind Texas state courts.
why.ga_app: Georgia sits in the Eleventh Circuit, not the Fifth. A sister circuit’s interpretation of a federal regulation is often persuasive and worth citing, but it carries no mandatory weight outside its own circuit.

authorities.label: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Eleventh Circuit (interpreting the federal regulation)

why.tx_state: The Eleventh Circuit covers Georgia, Alabama, and Florida — not Texas. Its regulatory interpretations carry no binding authority in the Texas state system.
why.ga_app: When a Georgia court applies a federal regulation, the Eleventh Circuit’s interpretation of that regulation is the authoritative federal word within this jurisdiction. Georgia courts treat it as binding on the federal question.

authorities.label: U.S. Supreme Court decision (interpreting the same federal regulation)

why.tx_state: On questions of federal law — including federal regulations — the U.S. Supreme Court is the final authority. Every court in the country, state and federal, must follow its interpretations.
why.ga_app: The U.S. Supreme Court settles federal questions for all courts everywhere. Its interpretation of a federal regulation ends the inquiry in any courtroom in the country.

why.tx_state: Another state’s supreme court has no authority in Texas. Georgia decisions are persuasive references, nothing more.
why.ga_app: The Georgia Supreme Court is Georgia’s highest court. Its decisions bind all Georgia courts below it, including the Court of Appeals — even when the issue before the lower court involves federal regulation.
